executive_summary: March 2026 paints a picture of two investor groups drifting apart in notable ways. Institutional investors now unanimously believe the market would bounce back quickly from a bad day — a striking 100% Buy-on-Dips reading — while individual investors have grown gloomier on nearly every front. Only a third of individuals expect stocks to be higher a year from now, and crash fears among everyday wealthy investors remain elevated. The mood, in short, is split: professionals appear resolute, while individuals seem rattled.

key_findings.headline: Individuals Turn Notably Pessimistic
key_findings.interpretation: Only a third of individual investors expect the market to rise over the next year, down sharply from about 58% in February. Institutional investors held steady at 50% — a coin-flip outlook — which may suggest they see roughly balanced risks ahead.

key_findings.headline: Crash Fears Linger Unevenly
key_findings.interpretation: Only 20% of individual investors feel confident a crash won't happen in the next six months, matching January's low. Institutional confidence edged down slightly from 54% to 50%, suggesting professionals are a bit less worried than everyday investors but still far from relaxed.

key_findings.headline: Professionals Fully Embrace Rebounds
key_findings.interpretation: Every single institutional respondent in March believes a sharp one-day drop would recover within two days — a perfect 100% reading. Meanwhile, individual investors swung hard in the opposite direction, falling from 81% to 40%, one of the widest gaps we've seen between the two groups.

key_findings.headline: Overvaluation Worries Persist
key_findings.interpretation: Six in ten individual investors believe the market is overvalued, though that's a modest improvement from February's extreme 50% reading. It's worth noting that some institutional investors — particularly those who subscribe to efficient-market views — may regard the overvaluation question as moot, since in their framework the current price is, by definition, the right price.

detailed_analysis:
The most eye-catching number this month is the Buy-on-Dips split. Institutional investors hit a perfect 100 — every single one expects a quick market bounce after a bad day. Individual investors, meanwhile, dropped from 81% to 40%. Think of it like two passengers on the same flight: one is napping comfortably, the other is gripping the armrest at every bump. This could reflect professionals' longer experience with market recoveries, or it could mean they're underestimating a genuinely new risk environment. Neither reading is automatically "right."

The 1-Year Outlook tells a quieter but consistent story. Institutional confidence held flat at 50%, while individual confidence fell from roughly 58% to 33%. That's a meaningful drop — it means only about one in three wealthy individual investors expects to see gains over the coming year. One reading of this: everyday investors may be reacting more viscerally to recent headlines or portfolio losses. Another possibility is that professionals are anchored to models and base rates that smooth out short-term anxiety.

Crash worry remains a persistent background hum, especially among individuals. At 20%, the individual Crash Confidence reading is as low as it was in January, meaning roughly four out of five wealthy individuals think a crash is at least plausible in the next six months. Institutional investors are more sanguine at 50%, but that still means half of professional money managers aren't willing to rule out a crash. This is not a market where either group feels truly safe.

Valuation Confidence ticked up modestly for both groups compared to February, but remains subdued overall. Only 40% of individual investors think the market is fairly valued or cheap, and just half of institutional investors agree. For those who take an efficient-market perspective, of course, this question is almost philosophical — the market price already reflects all available information. But for everyone else, the persistent sense that stocks are expensive may help explain the broader caution in this month's numbers.
market_context: [PLACEHOLDER: Add 2-3 sentences about recent market news relevant to this month's readings — e.g. Fed decisions, major earnings, economic data, or geopolitical events.]
methodology: These indices come from the Yale/Shiller U.S. Stock Market Confidence Surveys, which have tracked investor sentiment since 1989. Each month, roughly 100 wealthy individual investors and 100 institutional investment managers answer the same standardized questions. The consistency of those questions over 35+ years is what makes the data meaningful — you can actually compare investor psychology across very different market environments. The four indices each measure something distinct: • 1-Year Confidence: The share who expect prices to be higher in 12 months. • Crash Confidence: The share who believe a crash in the next 6 months is very unlikely (under 10% probability). A low reading means most people think a crash is plausible. • Buy-on-Dips Confidence: The share who believe that after a 3% single-day market drop, prices would fully recover within two days. • Valuation Confidence: The share who do not believe the market is overvalued. Surveys were conducted semi-annually before 2001 and have been monthly since July 2001. Respondents rotate over time, but consistent characteristics — high income, investment experience — are maintained across the panel.
